docs.title: Adyen Issuing SDKs
docs.text: A suite of Android SDKs for the Adyen Issuing platform.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Adyen Issuing Android SDKs let you embed cardholder-facing experiences — adding cards to digital wallets, revealing card details, and managing PINs — directly inside your application, with minimal sensitive data flowing through your own systems. Each SDK targets a specific domain and integrates with the matching backend APIs on the Adyen platform.

docs.title: Issuing SDKs
docs.text:
• Network Token Provisioning (available) — add Adyen-issued payment cards to digital wallets.
    • Session-based Provisioning SDK — session-scoped variant, published on Maven Central. Recommended for new integrations.
    • Offline Provisioning SDK — direct-integration variant, published on Maven Central
  • PIN & PAN Reveal (coming soon) — reveal card details, reveal PIN, and change PIN from within your app, without sensitive values passing through your own code.

docs.title: Network Token Provisioning
docs.text: Add Adyen-issued payment cards to digital wallets directly from your Android application.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Network token provisioning lets cardholders add a payment card from your app into a digital wallet (Google Wallet today, others in the future). The SDKs in this family handle the eligibility check, exchange opaque payment data with the wallet's push-provisioning API, and surface the final state back to your app — your code never touches the underlying card credentials.

Two SDK variants are offered. Pick the one that fits your backend integration.

docs.title: SDKs in this family
docs.text:
• Session-based Provisioning SDK — recommended for new integrations

    A session-scoped variant where the SDK manages the back-and-forth with Adyen on your behalf, reducing the surface area your backend has to expose.

    Open the SDK guide →

  • Offline Provisioning SDK

    The original integration: your backend exchanges sdkInput / sdkOutput opaque blobs with Adyen, and the SDK drives the wallet flow on the device. Full control over network calls and credential handling.

    Open the SDK guide →

docs.title: Choosing between them
docs.text: Concern Session-based Provisioning SDK Offline Provisioning SDK Backend → Adyen calls One session-token request per session One GET and one POST networkTokenActivationData call per card Application certificate Required (Adyen-issued .cer, embedded in res/raw) Not required Multi-card per instance Yes — one session covers a Set<String> of payment instruments No — one CardProvisioning instance per card Tamper detection signals Built in — TamperError result variant Not surfaced Migration From the Offline Provisioning SDK From pre-0.4.0

docs.title: Development & testing
docs.text:
Both SDKs enforce the same runtime environment constraints:

  • ❌ Emulator — SDK operations return errors
  • ✅ Device (debug or release build) — full end-to-end testing

docs.title: Example App
docs.text:
This example application demonstrates how to integrate both Adyen Issuing Network Token Provisioning SDKs to add cards to Google Wallet from your Android app:

  • The Session-based Provisioning SDK — session-scoped variant where the SDK manages the back-and-forth with Adyen behind a session token.
  • The Offline Provisioning SDK — direct-integration variant where your backend exchanges sdkInput / sdkOutput opaque blobs with Adyen.

A single Compose screen exercises whichever SDK is selected at runtime via a segmented-button toggle at the bottom of the main screen.

⚠️ Reference only — not functional end-to-end. The app runs against in-memory fakes and a placeholder application certificate, so the wallet-add step always fails. See Important notes for what must be replaced for a live integration.

docs.title: Session-based Provisioning SDK demo
docs.text:
The integration is implemented in the provisioningsessions/ package and exposed through ProvisioningSessionsRepository:

  1. Embed an application certificate. MainActivity loads it from res/raw/fake_app_cert and passes the input stream into the repository.
  2. Create a session. ProvisioningSession.create(...) is invoked with the certificate, the set of payment-instrument IDs, an activity provider, and a sessionTokenProvider lambda that calls SessionsBackend.fetchToken(paymentInstrumentIds) (your backend would obtain a session token via Adyen's session API).
  3. Check eligibility. session.canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId) returns the same CanBeProvisioned / CannotBeProvisioned.* shape as the direct SDK and is mapped onto the shared CardState.
  4. Provision the card. session.provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId, cardDisplayName, cardAddress) runs the wallet flow without further per-step backend involvement.

FakeSessionsBackend returns a static base64 session token, so the wallet-add step also fails by design with mock data.

docs.title: Offline Provisioning SDK demo
docs.text:
The integration is implemented in the provisioningsdk/ package and exposed through ProvisioningSdkRepository:

  1. Fetch activation data. SdkBackend.requestCardActivation(paymentInstrumentId) returns the opaque sdkInput blob your backend would receive from Adyen (GET .../paymentInstruments/{id}/networkTokenActivationData).
  2. Initialise the SDK. CardProvisioning.create(sdkInput, activityProvider) builds a CardProvisioning instance.
  3. Check provisioning eligibility. canProvision() resolves to CanBeProvisioned, AlreadyExistsInWallet, an ApiError / Error / UnknownFailure, and is mapped onto the shared CardState.
  4. Provision the card. provision(...) is invoked with an opcProvider lambda that re-enters the backend (requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput)) using the sdkOutput to fetch the matching Opaque Payment Card data; the lambda returns it to the SDK, which completes the wallet add.

FakeSdkBackend returns a static base64 sdkInput and a placeholder OPC string, so the wallet-add step intentionally fails — the demo wires the call sequence and result mapping correctly, but the data is not valid for live provisioning.

docs.title: Key components
docs.text: File Role MainActivity.kt Sets up the Compose UI; loads R.raw.fake_app_cert; provides the Activity reference to both SDKs. viewmodel/MainViewModel.kt Holds both repositories; dispatches getCardState(...) and provisionCard(...) to whichever SDK is selected. viewmodel/MainViewModelFactory.kt Wires the fakes (FakeSdkBackend, FakeSessionsBackend) into the repositories. data/ProvisioningMethod.kt Enum with SDK and SESSIONS entries; controls runtime dispatch. data/CardState.kt UI-facing state union (Loading, NotAddedToWallet, Provisioning, AddedToWallet, NotSupported, Disabled, Error) shared across both demos. repository/ProvisioningRepository.kt Common interface implemented by both per-SDK repositories. provisioningsdk/repository/ProvisioningSdkRepository.kt Offline Provisioning SDK integration. provisioningsessions/repository/ProvisioningSessionsRepository.kt Session-based Provisioning SDK integration. provisioningsdk/backend/SdkBackend.kt + provisioningsdk/fake/FakeSdkBackend.kt Backend interface + in-memory fake for the direct SDK flow. provisioningsessions/backend/SessionsBackend.kt + provisioningsessions/fake/FakeSessionsBackend.kt Backend interface + in-memory fake for the Sessions flow. ui/MainScreen.kt Compose UI; the segmented-button row at the bottom toggles between SDK and SESSIONS.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
1. Get the Google Push Provisioning API artifact. Both SDKs depend on com.google.android.gms:play-services-tapandpay, which is not on any public Maven repository. Follow the family-level Google Push Provisioning API Access guide.
  2. Clone the repository.
  3. Open the project in Android Studio.
  4. Build and run the example module on an Android device or emulator with Google Play Services. Use the segmented button at the bottom of the main screen to switch between the SDK and Sessions demos.

docs.title: Important notes
docs.text:
• Mock data. Tapping "Add to Google Wallet" produces an error in either mode — the mocked sdkInput / OPC / session token are syntactically valid placeholders but are not accepted by Adyen's or Google's live infrastructure.
  • Backend integration. FakeSdkBackend and FakeSessionsBackend are demo-only. In production you must implement real backend services that proxy to Adyen's Network Token APIs (for the SDK flow) or obtain session tokens via Adyen's session API (for the Sessions flow).
  • Application certificate. The bundled res/raw/fake_app_cert is a placeholder. The Sessions SDK requires a real application certificate provisioned by Adyen for production use.
  • Security. Never hardcode sensitive card data, OPC payloads, or session tokens in your application; always fetch them from your backend over an authenticated channel.
  • App architecture. The architecture, project structure, coding conventions, and UI design here are illustrative only — they don't represent required or preferred patterns from Adyen.

docs.title: Google Push Provisioning API Access
docs.text:
Both SDKs in the Network Token Provisioning family depend on Google's Push Provisioning API (com.google.android.gms:play-services-tapandpay version 18.8.0), which is not published to any public Maven repository. Before integrating either SDK, your app must be allowlisted by Google and you must obtain the artifact through a private channel.

The steps below are a one-time setup for your project; complete them once and the resulting allowlist entry and local artifact apply to whichever SDK you integrate.

docs.title: 1. Get your app allowlisted by Google
docs.text:
Your Adyen Project Operations Manager or Implementation Engineer can initiate this, or you can request allowlisting directly from Google. You will need to provide:

  • Your app's package name(s)
  • The SHA-1 signing certificate fingerprint for every build variant that will use the SDK

Note

Allowlisting is per package name + signing certificate. Add every developer machine certificate, every CI signing certificate, and every release certificate that will produce builds calling into the SDK.

docs.title: Finding your app's package name
docs.text:
The package name is the applicationId declared in your app module's build.gradle.kts (or build.gradle):

android {
    defaultConfig {
        applicationId = "com.example.yourapp"
        // …
    }
}

If you use product flavors or per-build-type applicationIdSuffixes, every resulting applicationId must be allowlisted separately.

docs.title: Finding the SHA-1 signing certificate fingerprint
Developer and CI builds (local keystores)
docs.text:
Run the signingReport Gradle task against your app module — either from Android Studio's Gradle pane (:app → Tasks → android → signingReport) or from a terminal:

./gradlew :app:signingReport

The task prints MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-256 fingerprints for each variant + keystore it can resolve. Copy the SHA-1 value for every variant you need allowlisted (typically debug for each developer machine, plus any release variant signed with a CI or release keystore).

docs.title: Google Play app signing (Play Console)
docs.text:
If you publish through Google Play and use Play App Signing, Google re-signs your release builds with an app signing key they manage. The SHA-1 of that key is not in your local keystore — you must retrieve it from the Play Console:

  1. Open the Play Console and select your app.
  2. Navigate to Test and release → Setup → App integrity → App signing.
  3. Under App signing key certificate, copy the SHA-1 certificate fingerprint. This is the fingerprint to allowlist for end-user devices that install your app from Google Play.

The same screen also lists the upload key certificate fingerprint (matching the keystore you sign uploads with). You do not need that for end-user allowlisting, but it can be useful when reconciling CI keystores.

docs.title: 2. Obtain the Push Provisioning API artifact
docs.text: Once your app is allowlisted, you can request the artifact from your Adyen Project Operations Manager or Implementation Engineer, or download it from Google.

docs.title: 3. Make the artifact available to your build
docs.text:
The artifact is delivered as a zip. The simplest setup is to unzip it into your local Maven cache at ~/.m2/repository/ so Gradle can resolve it via mavenLocal(). Alternatively, host the unzipped contents in an internal Maven repository your build can reach.

After unzipping and copying, the structure of the files in your local Maven directory should match the following Maven group / artifact / version layout:

~/.m2/repository/
└── com/
    └── google/
        └── android/
            └── gms/
                └── play-services-tapandpay/
                    └── 18.8.0/
                        ├── play-services-tapandpay-18.8.0.aar
                        └── play-services-tapandpay-18.8.0.pom

The exact file set may include additional Maven metadata (e.g. .module, checksum files) depending on how the artifact was packaged.

Then declare mavenLocal() in the repositories block of your settings.gradle.kts:

dependencyResolutionManagement {
    repositories {
        google()
        mavenCentral()
        mavenLocal()
    }
}

Important

The Google Push Provisioning API must not be published to a public Maven repository. Google's terms restrict it to allowlisted apps only.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Common issues encountered when integrating the Network Token Provisioning SDKs (the Session-based Provisioning SDK and the Offline Provisioning SDK), and how to resolve them.

Unless otherwise noted, every entry on this page applies to both SDKs — the underlying Google Tap and Pay integration, allowlisting requirements, and Google Wallet behaviour are shared.

docs.title: Google Tap and Pay dependency not found
docs.text:
The Network Token Provisioning SDKs have a transitive dependency on com.google.android.gms:play-services-tapandpay (18.8.0 or higher). This artifact is not published to any public Maven repository — it must be obtained through the allowlisting process and made available to your build via your local Maven cache or an internal repository.

If your build fails to resolve play-services-tapandpay, see Google Push Provisioning API Access for the full setup walkthrough.

docs.title: API calls fail with error code 15009
docs.text:
A 15009 status code returned from a Google Tap and Pay API call indicates the calling app is not allowlisted by Google for the Push Provisioning API. Verify that the package name and the SHA-1 signing certificate fingerprint of the build you are running have both been registered through the Push Provisioning API allowlist process.

The allowlist is per package + signing certificate, so each developer-local build, CI build, and release build must be enrolled separately. See Google Push Provisioning API Access for the full prerequisites.

docs.title: Provisioning fails with GooglePayNotSupported
docs.text: Provisioning calls return a GooglePayNotSupported result when Google Pay support is not configured for the payment card. Check the card's network or BIN range configuration.

docs.title: canProvision returns a CanBeProvisioned result after the card has already been provisioned
docs.text:
If a canProvision call reports the card as provisionable for a card that has already been added to Google Wallet, this is almost always an issuer-side configuration problem with the card scheme rather than an SDK bug. The most common root cause is a misconfigured application id at the scheme level.

Validate by attempting to add the card to Google Wallet manually (outside the SDK). If manual provisioning shows the card as provisionable as well, the scheme-side configuration needs to be corrected before push provisioning can return the right state.

docs.title: "Something went wrong, please try again" during the provisioning flow
docs.text:
A generic Google Wallet error of the form "Something went wrong, please try again" surfaced during a provisioning flow is also typically a card-configuration problem rather than an integration fault.

A quick way to confirm: try to manually provision the payment instrument (without going through the SDK). If manual provisioning fails with the same generic error, push provisioning is expected to fail too — the fix is on the card-configuration side.

docs.title: Tamper detection (TamperError) — Session-based Provisioning SDK only
docs.text:
The Session-based Provisioning SDK performs Runtime Application Self-Protection (RASP) checks at sensitive call sites. When a threat is detected (root, hooking, memory scanning, emulator, virtual environment, compromised hardware, or a debugger attached to a release build), the SDK halts sensitive work on the client side and returns a TamperError result.

Treat a TamperError as a signal to invalidate the user's session on your backend so the session token is refused regardless of what the compromised client does next, and surface a neutral error in the UI without hinting at the reason. The full rationale and an end-to-end example are covered in the Security Guide → Respond to Tamper Signals.

docs.title: Adyen Offline Provisioning SDK
docs.text: Integrate Adyen payment card provisioning into Google Wallet from your Android application.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Adyen Offline Provisioning SDK adds payment cards issued through Adyen to Google Wallet directly from your app. It handles the end-to-end provisioning flow — eligibility checks, opaque payment data exchange with Google's Push Provisioning API, and reporting the final state back to your app.

The integration unfolds in four steps: your backend fetches activation data, the SDK is initialised against that data, eligibility is checked, and the card is provisioned (with an in-flight callback that lets the SDK fetch Opaque Payment Card data via your backend).

sequenceDiagram
    box MOBILE DEVICE
        participant APP
        participant SDK
    end

    box SERVER SIDE
        participant BACKEND
        participant ADYEN
    end

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 1 — Get Activation Data
    APP->>BACKEND: Get Card Activation Data
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: GET /networkTokenActivationData
    activate ADYEN
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: activationData
    deactivate ADYEN
    BACKEND-->>APP: activationData
    deactivate BACKEND

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 2 — Initialize Provisioning Client
    APP->>SDK: CardProvisioning.create(activationData)

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 3 — Check if Can Add to Wallet
    APP->>SDK: canProvision()
    SDK-->>APP: CanBeProvisioned

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 4 — Provision the Card
    APP->>SDK: provision(activationData)
    activate SDK

    Note right of SDK: Callback initiated by SDK
    SDK->>APP: Request OPC Data
    activate APP
    APP->>BACKEND: Fetch OPC Data
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: POST /networkTokenActivationData
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: opaqueCardData
    BACKEND-->>APP: opaqueCardData
    deactivate BACKEND
    APP-->>SDK: opaqueCardData
    deactivate APP

    SDK-->>APP: ProvisionResult.Success
    deactivate SDK

docs.title: CardProvisioning
docs.text:
[Provisioning SDK]\ interface CardProvisioning

Provides methods to check if a given payment card can be added to Google Wallet and to add the card.

A separate instance of CardProvisioning is required for each payment card to be provisioned. CardProvisioning instances are created using the CardProvisioning.create function.

docs.text: //Adyen Provisioning Android SDK/com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.client/CardProvisioning/canProvision

docs.title: canProvision
docs.text:
[Provisioning SDK]\ abstract suspend fun canProvision(): CanProvisionResult

Confirms whether or not the card can be provisioned in Google Wallet.

docs.title: Return
docs.text: A CanProvisionResult object reporting whether or not the card can be provisioned and, if it cannot be provisioned, then specifying the reason.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
when (val result = cardProvisioning.canProvision()) {
    CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned -> {
        // Proceed to call `provision(...)`.
    }
    CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet -> {
        // Card is already in the active wallet.
    }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.ApiError -> {
        val statusCode: Int = result.statusCode
        // Tap & Pay API returned a non-zero status code.
    }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.Error -> {
        val cause: Throwable = result.throwable
        // Unexpected exception was thrown during the eligibility check.
    }
    CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.UnknownFailure -> {
        // Eligibility check failed without an exception.
    }
}

docs.title: provision
docs.text:
[Provisioning SDK]\ abstract suspend fun provision(cardDisplayName: String, cardAddress: CardAddress, opcProvider: OpcProvider): ProvisionResult

Attempts to provision the given card.

docs.title: Return
docs.text: a ProvisionResult Object confirming whether or not the card was provisioned correctly and, if not successfully provisioned, then specifying the reason for the failure.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Provisioning SDK

cardDisplayName The name on the card. Used to validate the card and also to display during the provisioning flow. cardAddress The address associated with the card. opcProvider The OpcProvider responsible for fetching OPC data.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
when (val result = cardProvisioning.provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider)) {
    is ProvisionResult.Success -> {
        val tokenId: String? = result.tokenId
        // Card was added to Google Wallet.
    }
    ProvisionResult.Failure.Canceled -> {
        // User cancelled the wallet flow.
    }
    is ProvisionResult.Failure.OpcFetchError -> {
        // The `OpcProvider` failed to fetch OPC data from the backend.
    }
    is ProvisionResult.Failure -> {
        // Inspect the failure variant for next steps. See `ProvisionResult.Failure`
        // for the full list of cases (network, attestation, internal errors, etc.).
    }
}

    is CardProvisioningCreateResult.Success -> {
        val cardProvisioning: CardProvisioning = result.cardProvisioning

    CardProvisioningCreateResult.Failed.GooglePayNotSupported -> {
        // Device does not support Google Pay — surface to the user.
    }
    CardProvisioningCreateResult.Failed.InvalidSdkInput -> {
        // The `sdkInput` blob from the backend was malformed.
    }
}

docs.title: OpcProvider
docs.text:
[Provisioning SDK]\ fun interface OpcProvider

Interface for retrieving opaque card data from the client's backend.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
OpcProvider { paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput ->
    // Forward `paymentInstrumentId` and `sdkOutput` to your backend, which in turn
    // calls the Adyen `POST /networkTokenActivationData` endpoint and returns the
    // Base64-encoded Opaque Payment Card (OPC) blob from the response. The SDK
    // hands the returned string straight to Google Tap & Pay — never decode or
    // mutate it on the device.
    httpClient.fetchOpc(
        paymentInstrumentId = paymentInstrumentId,
        sdkOutput = sdkOutput,
    )
}

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Integrate the Adyen Offline Provisioning SDK into your Android application.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before you begin:

  • Your application has a minSdk of 26 (Android 8.0) or higher.
  • Your application has a compileSdk of 35 or higher.
  • An API credential with the Network Token: Create role.
  • Your app ID is allowlisted by Google for the Push Provisioning API — see Google Push Provisioning API Access (shared across both SDKs in this family).

docs.title: Add the SDK
docs.text:
The SDK is published to Maven Central.

Add the dependency to your app module's build.gradle.kts:

dependencies {
    implementation("com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sdk:0.5.0-docs-preview")
}

Tip

Always check Maven Central for the latest version before integrating.

docs.title: Request Google Push Provisioning API Access
docs.text:
The SDK has a transitive dependency on Google's Push Provisioning API (com.google.android.gms:play-services-tapandpay), which is not published to any public Maven repository. Allowlisting your app and obtaining the artifact is a one-time setup that applies to either SDK in this family.

Follow the family-level guide: Google Push Provisioning API Access.

docs.title: Fetch Provisioning Data from Your Backend
docs.text:
Every provisioning flow begins with your backend fetching opaque sdkInput data from Adyen for a specific payment instrument.

From your backend, perform a GET request to:

https://{environment}.adyen.com/.../paymentInstruments/{paymentInstrumentId}/networkTokenActivationData

Headers:

  • Content-Type: application/json
  • X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY

The response contains an sdkInput string. Your backend returns this string to your Android app.

Note

The sdkInput is opaque — your app does not need to parse or interpret it. Forward it to the SDK as-is.

docs.title: Create a Provisioning Instance
docs.text:
With sdkInput in hand, create a CardProvisioning instance by calling the static factory:

val result = CardProvisioning.create(
    sdkInput = sdkInput,
    activityProvider = { cardIssuingActivity },
)

val cardProvisioning = when (result) {
    is CardProvisioningCreateResult.Success ->
        result.cardProvisioning
    is CardProvisioningCreateResult.Failed.GooglePayNotSupported -> {
        // Device does not support Google Pay; hide the "Add to Google Wallet" button
        return
    }
    is CardProvisioningCreateResult.Failed.InvalidSdkInput -> {
        // sdkInput is malformed; surface the error to your backend
        return
    }
}

The activityProvider lambda is invoked on demand during provisioning and must return the Activity that hosts your card-issuing UI. For single-Activity apps, return your main Activity.

Tip

Make sure the Activity returned by activityProvider is the currently-resumed host. The provisioning flow may outlive any single Activity instance if the user backgrounds your app, so avoid capturing a stale reference.

docs.title: Check Whether a Card Can Be Provisioned
docs.text:
Before showing an "Add to Google Wallet" affordance, ask the SDK whether the device and Google Wallet account can accept this card:

val canProvision = cardProvisioning.canProvision() == CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned

canProvision() is a suspend function — call it from a coroutine. The result reflects Google Wallet's view of the device + card combination at the moment of the call; treat it as advisory and re-check if the user revisits the screen later.

Note

canProvision() returns CanBeProvisioned only when the card is eligible and not already in the wallet. If the card has already been added on this device, you will get CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet. Surface that state by hiding or replacing the "Add to Google Wallet" button rather than re-attempting provisioning.

docs.title: Provision a Card
docs.text: When the user taps "Add to Google Wallet", drive the provisioning flow in two parts: implement an OpcProvider that knows how to fetch Opaque Payment Card (OPC) data from your backend on demand, then call provision() with the cardholder name, a CardAddress, and the provider.

docs.title: Implement an OpcProvider
docs.text:
The SDK calls OpcProvider.fetchOpc mid-flow once it has obtained an sdkOutput value to send to Adyen. Your implementation forwards sdkOutput to your backend, which performs a POST against /paymentInstruments/{paymentInstrumentId}/networkTokenActivationData with sdkOutput in the body, and returns the resulting OPC string back to the SDK.

val opcProvider = OpcProvider { paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput ->
    // Forward sdkOutput to your backend, which calls Adyen and returns the OPC string.
    // POST /paymentInstruments/{paymentInstrumentId}/networkTokenActivationData
    // Body: { "sdkOutput": sdkOutput }
    val opc: String = backend.fetchOpaquePaymentCard(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput)
    opc
}

The lambda is invoked on a background dispatcher; you can perform blocking network work directly inside it. If the call fails, throw — the SDK catches the exception and surfaces it as a ProvisionResult.Failure to your caller.

docs.title: Call provision()
docs.text:
val result = cardProvisioning.provision(
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
    opcProvider = opcProvider,
)

when (result) {
    is ProvisionResult.Success -> handleProvisioningSuccess(result.tokenId)
    is ProvisionResult.Failure -> handleProvisioningFailure(result)
}

provision() is a suspend function and presents Google's native "Add to Wallet" UI as part of its execution; call it from the Activity lifecycle (e.g. inside a lifecycleScope.launch { ... } triggered by the user's button tap).

Note

Passing an empty CardAddress() defers the address-entry step to Google Wallet's native flow — the user will be prompted to enter their address during provisioning. If you already have a verified address for the cardholder, populate the CardAddress fields to skip that step.

docs.title: Migrating from SDK versions prior to 0.4.0
docs.text:
Guidance for upgrading existing integrations from provisioning-sdk 0.3.x and earlier to 0.4.0+.

Migrating straight to the Session-based Provisioning SDK?

If you are on < 0.4.0 and your end goal is the Session-based Provisioning SDK, you can skip this migration entirely. The Session-based Provisioning SDK uses provisioning-sdk 0.4.0+ internally and exposes a different client API — the createSdkOutput() / OpcProvider / direct-SDK distinctions never surface in your code. Follow the Session-based Provisioning SDK — Getting Started instead. This page is for integrations that intend to stay on the Offline Provisioning SDK.

docs.title: Why upgrade
docs.text:
The primary reason to move to 0.4.0+ is forward compatibility with Google's Unified Push Provisioning (UPP).

provisioning-sdk — including the current 0.4.0+ line — is still implemented on top of Google's legacy Tap and Pay push-provisioning API. Google has marked that legacy API as deprecated and is replacing it with Unified Push Provisioning, which supports saving cards to a user's Google account rather than only to the specific device that ran the provisioning flow. Adyen will swap the SDK's internals over to UPP in a future release; that release will be designed as a drop-in version bump for clients already on the 0.4.0+ client API. Clients still on < 0.4.0 at that point will face an additional breaking-change migration on top of the SDK upgrade, because the pre-0.4.0 createSdkOutput() + provision(sdkInput, …) API cannot be preserved on top of UPP. Migrating now decouples your code from that future transition.

The 0.4.0+ API is built around a single OpcProvider callback that the SDK invokes when it requires the Opaque Payment Card (OPC) data, rather than requiring the application to orchestrate that exchange. Pre-0.4.0 integrations call createSdkOutput(), send the resulting sdkOutput to the backend to retrieve the OPC value from Adyen, and only then call provision(sdkInput, cardDisplayName, cardAddress). From 0.4.0 you pass an OpcProvider to a single provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider) call, and the SDK performs the round-trip internally.

Secondary benefits picked up along the way:

  • Fewer steps at the call site. One provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider) call replaces the two-stage createSdkOutput → backend call → provision(sdkInput, …) sequence.
  • Backend integration is unchanged. The two networkTokenActivationData endpoints (GET for sdkInput, POST for OPC) still carry the same payloads — only the timing of their invocation from the client changes.
  • Unified error handling. Failures inside the OPC retrieval surface through the single ProvisionResult returned from the new overload, rather than being split across CreateSdkOutputResult and ProvisionResult.

docs.title: Breaking changes
docs.text:
The two pre-0.4.0 entry points are now deprecated. Both still compile but emit warnings, and both will be removed in a future release.

API Status from 0.4.0 Replacement CardProvisioning.createSdkOutput(): CreateSdkOutputResult @Deprecated Not needed — the new provision(...) overload performs the equivalent step internally. CardProvisioning.provision(sdkInput, cardDisplayName, cardAddress): ProvisionResult @Deprecated (ReplaceWith("provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider)")) provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider): ProvisionResult CreateSdkOutputResult (sealed interface and all Success / Failure.* variants) @Deprecated Failure cases that previously surfaced here now flow through ProvisionResult.Failure.

The IDE quick-fix on the deprecated provision(...) overload offers the structural rewrite via the ReplaceWith hint, but you must additionally supply an OpcProvider implementation that performs the existing OPC backend call.

docs.title: OpcProvider interface
docs.text:
OpcProvider is a fun interface (single abstract method, so it accepts a SAM lambda):

public fun interface OpcProvider {
    public suspend fun fetchOpc(
        paymentInstrumentId: String,
        sdkOutput: String,
    ): String
}

The SDK invokes fetchOpc once per provision(...) call, passing the paymentInstrumentId of the card being provisioned and a newly issued sdkOutput. The implementation must return the base64-encoded OPC string from the backend. Exceptions thrown from fetchOpc propagate as a ProvisionResult.Failure, allowing retries and error mapping to be handled alongside the rest of the provisioning error handling.

docs.title: 1. Replace createSdkOutput() callers with an OpcProvider
docs.text:
Anywhere the OPC round-trip was previously invoked manually:

val sdkOutputResult = cardProvisioning.createSdkOutput()
val sdkOutput = (sdkOutputResult as CreateSdkOutputResult.Success).sdkOutput
val opc = backend.requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput).opc

…relocate the same backend call into a lambda that the SDK invokes on your behalf:

val opcProvider = OpcProvider { paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput ->
    backend.requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput).opc
}

If OpcProvider is the only argument requiring SAM conversion at the call site, it can also be inlined as a named lambda — see step 2.

docs.title: 2. Update the provision(...) call site
docs.text:
Before — pre-0.4.0 sequence with manual OPC orchestration:

// 1. Request sdkOutput from the SDK.
val sdkOutputResult = cardProvisioning.createSdkOutput()
val sdkOutput = (sdkOutputResult as CreateSdkOutputResult.Success).sdkOutput

// 2. Call the backend to exchange sdkOutput for the OPC value.
val opc = backend.requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput).opc

// 3. Pass the OPC back to the SDK to complete provisioning.
val provisionResult = cardProvisioning.provision(
    sdkInput = opc,
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
)

After — single call with an OpcProvider:

val provisionResult = cardProvisioning.provision(
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
    opcProvider = { paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput ->
        backend.requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput).opc
    },
)

The example app's ProvisioningSdkRepository demonstrates the same pattern integrated with a real backend client.

docs.title: 3. Remove the now-unused CreateSdkOutputResult handling
docs.text: Any when expression that exhausted CreateSdkOutputResult.Success / CreateSdkOutputResult.Failure.* branches can be removed entirely. Failures that previously surfaced there are now reflected in the ProvisionResult returned by the new provision(...) — update the existing ProvisionResult mapping if it does not already cover those cases.

docs.title: 4. Update the dependency version
docs.text:
Update the published version in the module's build.gradle.kts:

dependencies {
    implementation("com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sdk:0.5.0-docs-preview")
}

The deprecated APIs remain on the binary surface, so a transitive consumer that has not yet migrated will still link successfully — but new code on the 0.4.0+ API has no compile-time dependency on createSdkOutput() or the three-argument provision(...).

docs.title: What remains unchanged
docs.text:
• CardProvisioning.create(sdkInput, activityProvider) — unchanged. The sdkInput returned by the GET /networkTokenActivationData backend call is still passed at construction time.
  • canProvision() — unchanged signature and CanProvisionResult variants.
  • CardAddress — same package, same fields.
  • Backend endpoints — the two networkTokenActivationData calls are still issued; only the timing of their invocation on the client changes.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:

  • Getting Started — current integration guide for new projects.
  • Offline Provisioning SDK → Session-based Provisioning SDK migration — separate, optional migration from the 0.4.0+ Offline Provisioning SDK to the Session-based Provisioning SDK. Not a prerequisite of, or a follow-on to, the migration on this page.

docs.title: Adyen Session-based Provisioning SDK
docs.text: Session-scoped network token provisioning for Adyen-issued payment cards.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Session-based Provisioning SDK wraps the same Google Push Provisioning flow as the Offline Provisioning SDK, but mediates the back-and-forth with Adyen behind a session token. Your backend obtains a session token via Adyen's session API and hands it to the SDK; the SDK then drives the entire provisioning flow without further backend involvement per step.

The result is a smaller backend surface area and a simpler client integration — at the cost of being slightly less flexible than the Offline Provisioning SDK if you need step-by-step control.

The integration unfolds in three steps: your backend obtains a session token, eligibility is checked through the session, and the card is provisioned. The SDK handles the rest of the protocol with Adyen behind the scenes.

sequenceDiagram
    box MOBILE DEVICE
        participant APP
        participant SDK
    end

    box SERVER SIDE
        participant BACKEND
        participant ADYEN
    end

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 1 — Get Session Token
    APP->>BACKEND: Request Session Token
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: Request Session Token
    activate ADYEN
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: Return Session Token
    deactivate ADYEN
    BACKEND-->>APP: Return Session Token
    deactivate BACKEND

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 2 — Check if Can Add to Wallet
    APP->>SDK: canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)
    activate SDK
    SDK-->>APP: CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned
    deactivate SDK

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 3 — Provision the Card
    APP->>SDK: provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId, ...)
    activate SDK
    SDK-->>APP: ProvisionResult.Success
    deactivate SDK

docs.title: ProvisioningSession
docs.text:
[Provisioning Sessions SDK]\ interface ProvisioningSession

Provides methods to establish a card provisioning session and to then allow checking if a given payment card can be added to Google Wallet and to allow adding the card.

A separate instance of ProvisioningSession is required for each set of payment cards to be provisioned. ProvisioningSession instances are created using the ProvisioningSession.create function.

docs.title: Can provision card
docs.text: //Adyen Provisioning Sessions Android SDK/com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.sessions/ProvisioningSession/canProvisionCard

docs.title: canProvisionCard
docs.text:
[Provisioning Sessions SDK]\ abstract suspend fun canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId: String): CanProvisionResult

Confirms whether or not a payment card can be provisioned in Google Wallet.

docs.title: Return
docs.text: A CanProvisionResult object reporting whether or not the card can be provisioned and, if it cannot be provisioned, then specifying the reason.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Provisioning Sessions SDK

paymentInstrumentId The payment instrument id of the card to be provisioned.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
when (val result = session.canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)) {
    CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned -> {
        // Proceed to call `provisionCard(...)`.
    }
    CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet -> {
        // Card is already in the active wallet.
    }
    CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.PaymentInstrumentNotAllowed -> {
        // The id was not in the set passed to `ProvisioningSession.create(...)`.
    }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.TamperError -> {
        val tamperCode: Int = result.tamperCode
        // RASP violation detected — share `tamperCode` with Adyen support.
    }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned -> {
        // Inspect the failure variant for next steps (HTTP, encryption,
        // serialization, token provider, certificate, or unknown errors).
    }
}

docs.title: Provision card
docs.text: //Adyen Provisioning Sessions Android SDK/com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.sessions/ProvisioningSession/provisionCard

docs.title: provisionCard
docs.text:
[Provisioning Sessions SDK]\ abstract suspend fun provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId: String, cardDisplayName: String, cardAddress: CardAddress): ProvisionResult

Attempts to provision the given card.

docs.title: Return
docs.text: a ProvisionResult object confirming whether or not the card was provisioned correctly and, if not successfully provisioned, then specifying the reason for the failure.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Provisioning Sessions SDK

paymentInstrumentId The payment instrument id of the card to be provisioned. cardDisplayName The name on the card. Used to validate the card and also to display during the provisioning flow. cardAddress The address associated with the card.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
val result = session.provisionCard(
    paymentInstrumentId = paymentInstrumentId,
    cardDisplayName = cardDisplayName,
    cardAddress = cardAddress,
)
when (result) {
    is ProvisionResult.Success -> {
        val tokenId: String? = result.tokenId
        // Card was added to Google Wallet.
    }
    ProvisionResult.Failure.Canceled -> {
        // User cancelled the wallet flow.
    }
    is ProvisionResult.Failure.TamperError -> {
        val tamperCode: Int = result.tamperCode
        // RASP violation detected — share `tamperCode` with Adyen support.
    }
    is ProvisionResult.Failure -> {
        // Inspect the failure variant for next steps. See `ProvisionResult.Failure`
        // for the full list of cases.
    }
}

docs.title: Return
docs.text: A ProvisioningSessionCreateResult containing the session instance if successful, or a failure reason otherwise.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Provisioning Sessions SDK

context The application context. applicationCertificate A certificate provided to the client by Adyen and unique to each client application. paymentInstrumentIds The payment instrument ids of the cards that may be provisioned during the session. activityProvider A function which returns an Activity. The Activity returned should be the Activity which hosts the card provisioning functionality within the client application. sessionTokenProvider a function implemented by the client which returns a session token via the client backend and originating from the Adyen backend session provider. environment optional parameter allowing selection of a ProvisioningEnvironment. Default is ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE.

[Provisioning Sessions SDK]\ fun create(context: Context, appCertInputStream: InputStream, paymentInstrumentIds: Set<String>, activityProvider: () -> Activity, sessionTokenProvider: suspend (Set<String>) -> String, environment: ProvisioningEnvironment = ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE): ProvisioningSessionCreateResult

Creates a ProvisioningSession instance using an application certificate from the given InputStream.

docs.title: Return
docs.text: A ProvisioningSessionCreateResult containing the session instance if successful, or a failure reason otherwise.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text:
Provisioning Sessions SDK

context An application context. appCertInputStream An InputStream containing the application certificate. paymentInstrumentIds The payment instrument ids of the cards that may be provisioned during the session. activityProvider A function which returns an Activity. The Activity returned should be the Activity which hosts the card provisioning functionality within the client application. sessionTokenProvider a function implemented by the client which returns a session token via the client backend and originating from the Adyen backend session provider. environment optional parameter allowing selection of a ProvisioningEnvironment. Default is ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE.

docs.title: Samples
docs.text:
val result = ProvisioningSession.create(
    context = context,
    appCertInputStream = appCertInputStream,
    paymentInstrumentIds = paymentInstrumentIds,
    activityProvider = { activity },
    sessionTokenProvider = fetchSessionTokenFromBackend,
    environment = ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE,
)
when (result) {
    is ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Success -> {
        val session: ProvisioningSession = result.session
        // Use `session` to call `canProvisionCard(...)` and `provisionCard(...)`.
    }
    ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Failure.ApplicationCertificateError -> {
        // The supplied application certificate could not be parsed.
    }
    is ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Failure.Error -> {
        // Unexpected error during session creation.
    }
}

  • Your application has a compileSdk of 35 or higher.
  • An API credential with the Network Token: Create role.
  • Your app ID is allowlisted by Google for the Push Provisioning API — see Google Push Provisioning API Access (shared across both SDKs in this family).

In addition, the Session-based SDK requires an application certificate provided by Adyen — see below.

docs.title: Adyen Session-based Provisioning SDK application certificate
docs.text:
The application certificate is specific to your application and lets the SDK verify the host application at runtime. You can request it from your Adyen Project Operations Manager or Implementation Manager. You will need to provide your Android application's applicationId (look for the applicationId property in your main app module's build.gradle.kts).

If your applicationId ever changes, request a new certificate for the new id — the existing one will not match.

The certificate is provided as a file with a .cer extension. You will embed it in your app in step 1.

docs.title: 1. Embed the Certificate
docs.text:
Add the .cer file Adyen provided to your app module's res/raw resource directory (create it if you do not already have one). At runtime, load it into an InputStream:

// Load the certificate data from a raw resource named `app_cert`.
val appCertInputStream = context.resources.openRawResource(R.raw.app_cert)

You will pass this InputStream into ProvisioningSession.create() in step 4.

docs.title: 2. Fetch a Session Token
docs.text:
Each provisioning attempt is scoped to a session token your backend obtains from the Adyen API. From your backend, perform:

URL — https://test.adyen.com/authe/api/v1/sessions (use the live host for production).

Headers

  • Content-Type: application/json
  • X-API-Key: YOUR_API_KEY

Body

{
    "allowOrigin": "https://www.adyen.com",
    "product": "platform",
    "policy": {
        "resources": [
            { "type": "paymentInstrument", "paymentInstrumentId": "PI..." }
        ],
        "roles": ["Network Token: Create"]
    }
}

Have your backend return the resulting session token to your Android app on demand. The SDK will request a token via the sessionTokenProvider callback you provide in step 4.

docs.title: 3. Add the Session-based Provisioning SDK Dependency
docs.text:
The SDK is published to Maven Central. Add the dependency to your app module's build.gradle.kts:

dependencies {
    implementation("com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sessions:0.5.0-docs-preview")
}

Or the equivalent version catalog entry.

Tip

Always check Maven Central for the latest version before integrating.

docs.title: 4. Initialize the Provisioning Session
docs.text:
Create a ProvisioningSession via the static factory:

public fun create(
    context: Context,
    appCertInputStream: InputStream,
    paymentInstrumentIds: Set<String>,
    activityProvider: () -> Activity,
    sessionTokenProvider: suspend (Set<String>) -> String,
    environment: ProvisioningEnvironment = ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE,
): ProvisioningSessionCreateResult

Parameter rundown:

  • context — application or activity context.
  • appCertInputStream — the application-certificate InputStream from step 1.
  • paymentInstrumentIds — the payment instruments that may be provisioned during this session.
  • activityProvider — function returning the host Activity (typically the Activity hosting your card-provisioning screen, or your single-Activity app's main Activity).
  • sessionTokenProvider — suspend function the SDK invokes when it needs a session token (see step 2).
  • environment — defaults to ProvisioningEnvironment.LIVE. Switch to ProvisioningEnvironment.TEST for the Adyen test environment.

Note

There is also an overload that accepts a java.security.cert.X509Certificate directly if you would prefer to manage the certificate as an in-memory object rather than a stream.

A typical wiring up looks like:

suspend fun fetchSessionTokenFromBackend(paymentInstrumentIds: Set<String>): String {
    val body = buildSessionTokenRequestBody(paymentInstrumentIds) // see step 2
    val response = httpClient.post("yourBackend/sessionToken", body)
    if (!response.isSuccessful()) {
        // Throw — the SDK will surface the failure as a Failure result to your caller.
        throw IllegalStateException("Could not fetch session token: ${response.code}")
    }
    return response.bodyText()
}

val result = ProvisioningSession.create(
    context = applicationContext,
    appCertInputStream = appCertInputStream,
    paymentInstrumentIds = setOf("PI...01", "PI...02"),
    activityProvider = { provisioningActivity },
    sessionTokenProvider = { paymentInstrumentIds ->
        fetchSessionTokenFromBackend(paymentInstrumentIds)
    },
)

val provisioningSession = when (result) {
    is ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Success ->
        result.session
    is ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Failure.ApplicationCertificateError ->
        throw IllegalStateException("Invalid application certificate")
    is ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Failure.Error ->
        throw IllegalStateException("Unexpected initialization error")
}

The Failure variants distinguish between a malformed / mis-issued application certificate and any other unexpected initialization error.

docs.title: 5. Check if a Card Can Be Provisioned
docs.text:
Before showing an "Add to Google Wallet" affordance, ask the session whether each card can be added to the user's wallet:

when (val result = provisioningSession.canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)) {
    // Card is ready to be added to the wallet.
    is CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned ->
        showAddToWalletButton()
    // Card has already been added to this device's wallet.
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet ->
        hideAddToWalletButton()
    // Card cannot be added for some other reason.
    else ->
        showCannotBeProvisionedReason(result)
}

canProvisionCard() is a suspend function — call it from a coroutine. Treat the result as advisory and re-check if the user revisits the screen later.

docs.title: 6. Provision the Card
docs.text:
When the user taps "Add to Google Wallet", drive Google's native provisioning flow:

val provisionResult = provisioningSession.provisionCard(
    paymentInstrumentId = paymentInstrumentId,
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
)

provisionCard() is a suspend function and presents Google's native "Add to Wallet" UI as part of its execution. Call it from the Activity lifecycle (e.g. inside lifecycleScope.launch { ... } triggered by the user's tap).

Note

Passing an empty CardAddress() defers the address-entry step to Google Wallet's native flow — the user will be prompted to enter their address during provisioning. If you already have a verified address for the cardholder, populate the CardAddress fields to skip that step.

docs.title: 7. Handle the Final State
docs.text:
provisionCard() returns a ProvisionResult once the Google Wallet flow completes (successfully or otherwise). Branch on it to update your UI:

when (provisionResult) {
    is ProvisionResult.Success ->
        handleProvisioningSuccess(provisionResult.tokenId)
    is ProvisionResult.Failure ->
        handleProvisioningFailure(provisionResult)
}

The Failure hierarchy distinguishes error categories such as user cancellation, network problems, and the tamper signal covered next.

docs.title: 8. Respond to TamperError
docs.text:
Both canProvisionCard() and provisionCard() may return a TamperError variant when the SDK's Runtime Application Self-Protection (RASP) checks detect that the device has been compromised — root, hooking, memory scanning, emulator, virtual environment, compromised hardware, or a debugger attached to a release build.

When you receive a TamperError, treat it as a signal to invalidate the user's session on your backend. The SDK halts sensitive work on the client side, but it does not terminate the server-side session — that responsibility is delegated to you so the response composes with your wider fraud controls.

when (val result = provisioningSession.canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)) {
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.TamperError -> {
        // Revoke the session on your backend so the session token is refused
        // regardless of what the compromised client does next, then surface a
        // neutral error to the user.
        backend.invalidateSession()
        showGenericErrorAndLogOut()
    }
    // … handle other result variants
    else -> { /* … */ }
}

when (val result = provisioningSession.provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId, cardDisplayName, cardAddress)) {
    is ProvisionResult.Failure.TamperError -> {
        backend.invalidateSession()
        showGenericErrorAndLogOut()
    }
    // … handle other result variants
    else -> { /* … */ }
}

Do not surface the specific tamper reason to the user — a neutral error message avoids giving attackers feedback on which check triggered. The tamperCode on the result is intended for reporting to Adyen support, not for end-user display.

See Respond to Tamper Signals in the Security Guide for the full rationale, including why the client-side SDK does not self-wipe keys or cached state on detection.

docs.title: Migrating from the Offline Provisioning SDK to the Session-based Provisioning SDK
docs.text:
Guidance for moving an existing integration from the Offline Provisioning SDK to the Session-based Provisioning SDK.

The example app contains both integrations side-by-side under provisioningsdk/ and provisioningsessions/, sharing a single ProvisioningRepository interface — a useful concrete reference for the diff described below.

Coming from a < 0.4.0 Offline Provisioning SDK?

You don't need to do the pre-0.4 migration first. The "Before" snippets on this page show the post-0.4.0 Offline Provisioning SDK API so the diff to the Session-based Provisioning SDK is small, but if your existing integration is on < 0.4.0 you can replace it with the Session-based flow directly — none of the pre-0.4.0 createSdkOutput() / manual OPC orchestration carries over either way. Use the Session-based Provisioning SDK — Getting Started as your primary reference and treat the "Before" snippets here as illustrative of what the Offline Provisioning SDK call site looks like, not as a prerequisite your code must already match.

docs.title: Why migrate
docs.text:
Session-based provisioning consolidates several integration concerns that the Offline Provisioning SDK delegates to the backend:

  • Smaller backend surface area. A single session-token endpoint replaces the two-leg networkTokenActivationData exchange (GET for sdkInput, POST with sdkOutput for OPC). Once the session token is issued, the SDK handles the remainder of the provisioning protocol with Adyen internally.
  • Multi-card sessions. A single ProvisioningSession covers a Set<String> of payment-instrument IDs, so flows that present several cards at once (e.g. a wallet management screen) incur the session-creation cost only once.
  • Tamper-detection signals. The session pipeline surfaces device-tamper outcomes alongside the standard provisioning results, providing an integrated signal in client error handling rather than a separate workflow.
  • Simpler client lifecycle. No OpcProvider callback to integrate with the backend; no per-step opaque-payload handling in application code.

The trade-off is reduced step-by-step control. If there are integration-specific reasons to stage sdkInput / sdkOutput through application logic between canProvision and provision, the Offline Provisioning SDK remains the appropriate choice.

docs.title: Flow comparison
docs.text: The diagrams below visualise the two integrations end-to-end. The Sessions flow has one fewer numbered step and removes the SDK → app → backend OPC callback round-trip during provision(...).

docs.title: Offline Provisioning SDK — four-step flow with OPC callback
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    box MOBILE DEVICE
        participant APP
        participant SDK
    end

    box SERVER SIDE
        participant BACKEND
        participant ADYEN
    end

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 1 — Get Activation Data
    APP->>BACKEND: Get Card Activation Data
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: GET /networkTokenActivationData
    activate ADYEN
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: activationData
    deactivate ADYEN
    BACKEND-->>APP: activationData
    deactivate BACKEND

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 2 — Initialize Provisioning Client
    APP->>SDK: CardProvisioning.create(activationData)

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 3 — Check if Can Add to Wallet
    APP->>SDK: canProvision()
    SDK-->>APP: CanBeProvisioned

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 4 — Provision the Card
    APP->>SDK: provision(activationData)
    activate SDK

    Note right of SDK: Callback initiated by SDK
    SDK->>APP: Request OPC Data
    activate APP
    APP->>BACKEND: Fetch OPC Data
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: POST /networkTokenActivationData
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: opaqueCardData
    BACKEND-->>APP: opaqueCardData
    deactivate BACKEND
    APP-->>SDK: opaqueCardData
    deactivate APP

    SDK-->>APP: ProvisionResult.Success
    deactivate SDK

docs.title: Session-based Provisioning SDK — three-step flow with no per-call backend involvement
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    box MOBILE DEVICE
        participant APP
        participant SDK
    end

    box SERVER SIDE
        participant BACKEND
        participant ADYEN
    end

    Note over APP,ADYEN: Step 1 — Get Session Token
    APP->>BACKEND: Request Session Token
    activate BACKEND
    BACKEND->>ADYEN: Request Session Token
    activate ADYEN
    ADYEN-->>BACKEND: Return Session Token
    deactivate ADYEN
    BACKEND-->>APP: Return Session Token
    deactivate BACKEND

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 2 — Check if Can Add to Wallet
    APP->>SDK: canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)
    activate SDK
    SDK-->>APP: CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned
    deactivate SDK

    Note over APP,SDK: Step 3 — Provision the Card
    APP->>SDK: provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId, ...)
    activate SDK
    SDK-->>APP: ProvisionResult.Success
    deactivate SDK

docs.title: API mapping
docs.text:
Concern Offline Provisioning SDK Session-based Provisioning SDK Maven coordinate com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sdk com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sessions Primary type CardProvisioning (one instance per card) ProvisioningSession (one instance per card set) Factory CardProvisioning.create(sdkInput, activityProvider) ProvisioningSession.create(context, appCertInputStream, paymentInstrumentIds, activityProvider, sessionTokenProvider, environment = LIVE) Eligibility check canProvision(): CanProvisionResult canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId): CanProvisionResult Provision provision(cardDisplayName, cardAddress, opcProvider): ProvisionResult provisionCard(paymentInstrumentId, cardDisplayName, cardAddress): ProvisionResult Backend bridge OpcProvider lambda fetches OPC per call sessionTokenProvider: suspend (Set<String>) -> String creates once per session Application certificate Not required Required (appCertInputStream or pre-parsed X509Certificate) Environment selector Not exposed ProvisioningEnvironment (LIVE, BETA, TEST) Result types package com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.client.results.* com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.sessions.results.* Card address type com.adyen.issuing.mobile.provisioning.client.data.CardAddress Same type, re-used across both SDKs Activity provider () -> Activity Same signature Experimental opt-in @AdyenExperimentalApi Same annotation

The two SDKs use distinct results packages with different concrete variants, so any when expression that is exhaustive over the SDK's CanProvisionResult / ProvisionResult / CardProvisioningCreateResult branches will need to be updated to cover the Sessions counterparts. See Result-type expansion below.

docs.title: What goes away
docs.text:
The two networkTokenActivationData calls used by the direct SDK are no longer invoked from the client:

  • GET .../paymentInstruments/{id}/networkTokenActivationData — previously returned sdkInput.
  • POST .../paymentInstruments/{id}/networkTokenActivationData — previously returned the OPC payload in exchange for sdkOutput.

Once the migration is complete and the SDK consumers are removed, the corresponding backend endpoints can be decommissioned (or repurposed for any non-SDK use cases).

docs.title: What replaces them
docs.text:
A single endpoint on your backend that creates an Adyen session token for the set of payment-instrument IDs the user is about to operate on. Its response — a base64-encoded session token issued by Adyen's session API — is what your sessionTokenProvider lambda returns to the SDK.

The lambda signature is:

sessionTokenProvider: suspend (Set<String>) -> String

It receives the same paymentInstrumentIds set you passed to ProvisioningSession.create(...) and is expected to return the session token string.

Your backend obtains the token by calling Adyen's session API:

  • Live — POST https://authe-live.adyen.com/authe/api/v1/sessions
  • Test — POST https://authe-test.adyen.com/authe/api/v1/sessions

docs.title: Client code changes
docs.text: The example app demonstrates both call sequences side-by-side; the snippets below summarise the differences.

docs.title: Before — Offline Provisioning SDK
docs.text:
// Backend call 1: GET networkTokenActivationData -> sdkInput
val sdkInput: String = sdkBackend.requestCardActivation(paymentInstrumentId).sdkInput

val createResult = CardProvisioning.create(
    sdkInput = sdkInput,
    activityProvider = { hostingActivity },
)
val cardProvisioning = (createResult as CardProvisioningCreateResult.Success).cardProvisioning

when (cardProvisioning.canProvision()) {
    is CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned -> { /* show Add to Wallet */ }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet -> { /* hide button */ }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.ApiError,
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.Error,
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.UnknownFailure -> { /* show error */ }
}

val provisionResult = cardProvisioning.provision(
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
    opcProvider = { paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput ->
        // Backend call 2: POST networkTokenActivationData with sdkOutput -> OPC
        sdkBackend.requestOpaquePaymentCardData(paymentInstrumentId, sdkOutput).sdkInput
    },
)

docs.title: After — Session-based Provisioning SDK
docs.text:
val createResult = ProvisioningSession.create(
    context = applicationContext,
    appCertInputStream = resources.openRawResource(R.raw.app_cert),
    paymentInstrumentIds = setOf(paymentInstrumentId),
    activityProvider = { hostingActivity },
    sessionTokenProvider = { paymentInstrumentIds ->
        // Single backend call: the endpoint requests a token from Adyen's session API
        sessionsBackend.fetchToken(paymentInstrumentIds)
    },
)
val session = (createResult as ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Success).session

when (session.canProvisionCard(paymentInstrumentId)) {
    is CanProvisionResult.CanBeProvisioned -> { /* show Add to Wallet */ }
    is CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned.AlreadyExistsInWallet -> { /* hide button */ }
    // ... see Result-type expansion below for the additional variants to handle
}

val provisionResult = session.provisionCard(
    paymentInstrumentId = paymentInstrumentId,
    cardDisplayName = "John Doe",
    cardAddress = CardAddress(),
)

The CardAddress import does not change — it is defined in the Offline Provisioning SDK's client.data package and re-used unchanged by the Session-based Provisioning SDK.

docs.title: Result-type expansion
docs.text:
The Session-based result types intentionally expose a wider failure surface than the Offline Provisioning SDK because operational concerns the Offline Provisioning SDK delegates to your backend (token-fetch errors, certificate validation, tamper detection, transport / serialization failures) become observable inside the SDK once it manages the session.

Concrete additions to cover when updating exhaustive when expressions:

ProvisioningSessionCreateResult.Failure — replaces CardProvisioningCreateResult.Failed:

  • ApplicationCertificateError — the bytes you supplied could not be parsed as an X.509 certificate.
  • Error(message: String?) — generic creation failure with optional reason.

GooglePayNotSupported and InvalidSdkInput from the SDK no longer occur at session creation: GooglePayNotSupported moves to CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned, and InvalidSdkInput no longer exists because there is no opaque input payload.

Sessions CanProvisionResult.CannotBeProvisioned — supersedes the SDK's variants:

  • All four SDK variants (AlreadyExistsInWallet, ApiError, Error, UnknownFailure) plus a GooglePayNotSupported branch that previously surfaced at create-time.
  • New: DecryptError, EncryptError, HttpError, SdkCertificateError, InvalidApplicationCertificate, PaymentInstrumentNotAllowed, SerializationError, TokenProviderError, TamperError.

Sessions ProvisionResult.Failure — supersedes the SDK's variants:

  • New: AlreadyProvisioned, Canceled, DecryptError, EncryptError, Error, HttpError, SdkCertificateError, InvalidApplicationCertificate, PaymentInstrumentNotAllowed, ProvisioningInProgress, SerializationError, TokenProviderError, ProvisioningSdkFailure, TapAndPayApiFailure, TapAndPayNotAvailable, TamperError.

Most of these can be mapped to a small set of UI states for typical applications (retryable transport error, non-retryable validation error, terminal "card is already in wallet"), but the Kotlin compiler will require each branch to be addressed explicitly.

docs.title: What remains unchanged
docs.text:
• Google Push Provisioning API allowlisting. Both SDKs depend transitively on com.google.android.gms:play-services-tapandpay and the same allowlisting steps apply. See Google Push Provisioning API Access.
  • CardAddress. Same fields, same package, no migration required.
  • activityProvider: () -> Activity. The lambda is invoked in the same way; pass the host Activity as before.
  • @AdyenExperimentalApi. Both SDKs require the same opt-in annotation at call sites.
  • Minimum Android API and compileSdk requirements. Unchanged at API 26 / compileSdk 35.

docs.title: Step-by-step migration
docs.text:
1. Add the new dependency alongside the existing one so the migration can be shipped incrementally:

    dependencies {
    implementation("com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sdk:0.5.0-docs-preview") // existing
    implementation("com.adyen.issuing.provisioning:provisioning-sessions:0.5.0-docs-preview") // new
}

  2. Embed the application certificate. Place the certificate provided by Adyen in res/raw/ (or another readable location) so it can be passed to ProvisioningSession.create(...) as an InputStream.

  3. Implement the session-token backend endpoint that proxies to Adyen's session API and returns the issued token. Integrate it into the client behind a sessionTokenProvider lambda.

  4. Introduce a new repository or use-case that encapsulates the Session-based flow without disturbing the existing Offline Provisioning SDK code path. The example app's ProvisioningSessionsRepository (alongside the existing ProvisioningSdkRepository implementing the same ProvisioningRepository interface) is the reference layout.

  5. Switch call sites incrementally. A feature flag or runtime toggle (the example app uses a segmented button bound to a ProvisioningMethod enum) allows routing a percentage of traffic through the Session-based Provisioning SDK while leaving fallback to the Offline Provisioning SDK in place.

  6. Update the error mapping to cover the expanded CanProvisionResult and ProvisionResult branches. Treat the new *Error variants conservatively — retry where transport-related; otherwise surface the failure and abort.

  7. Decommission the old API surface once the Session-based Provisioning SDK is rolled out: remove the Offline Provisioning SDK dependency, delete the OpcProvider integration code, and retire the two networkTokenActivationData backend endpoints if no other consumers remain.
